title: Spencerian capital letters — the principal forms
subtitle: The seven principal capital forms of the Spencerian system with stroke order
excerpt: Spencerian capitals are among the most ornate in the pointed-pen tradition. Spencer's system groups all 26 letters into seven families, each sharing a principal stroke. This tutorial teaches the seven families with the stroke order for each representative letter.
sourceNotes: Platt Rogers Spencer, Spencerian Key to Practical Penmanship (1866); the Spencer Brothers' instructional copybooks (1868 onward, multiple editions). Both are in the public domain. The seven-family capital grouping is Spencer's own classification. Internet Archive, archive.org/search?query=spencerian.

glossaryTerms.definition: A system of pointed-pen writing developed by Platt Rogers Spencer in the 1850s and 1860s. Characterised by elegant oval forms, light hairlines, and ornate capital letters built from seven principal strokes.
In the Spencerian system, one of the seven foundational shapes from which all capital letters are derived. Each family of capitals shares the same principal stroke.

content.text: capital alphabet is built from seven

. Every capital letter belongs to one family, and once you can produce the principal stroke for a family, the capitals in that family become learnable as variations on that stroke. The capitals are written at 7 to 8 nib widths above the baseline, sloped at 52 degrees.

items.name: Pointed nib (Hunt 101 for advanced; Nikko G for beginners)
Hot-press watercolour paper, 200 gsm, or smooth cartridge

content.text: The seven principal strokes and their families
First principal: the large steam. Letters: I, J, T, F, H, K, M, N, V, X, Y, Z

The large stem is a reverse-curve downstroke from the ascender height to the baseline, with a slight shade in the middle. It begins with a small curl to the left at the top, curves right to gather the shade, and exits to the right at the base. I, J, and T are largely this stroke with minor additions. K adds a loop and lower diagonal. M adds two more strokes beside the stem.
Second principal: the direct oval. Letters: O, C, E, A

The direct oval starts at the 1 o'clock position, sweeps left and down in a shade, closes at the 5 o'clock position with a hairline re-entry. O is this oval closed at the top with a second hairline arc. A uses the oval as its left side, with an additional stem and crossbar.
Third principal: the indirect oval. Letters: D, P, B, R

The indirect oval curves from the 7 o'clock position up and to the right, shading on the downstroke from 12 o'clock to 6 o'clock, with the return hairline curving left. D uses this oval with a tall left-side stem. B has two indirect ovals stacked with the upper smaller. P uses only the upper oval on a long stem.
Fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh principals: extended forms

Fourth (the loop): used in L, G, S, and Q, which each carry a distinctive looped structure at the base or top. Fifth (the reverse curve): used in U and W, which open with a wide curve to the right before closing. Sixth (the angular or horizontal): used in E, F, and selected strokes on H and T. Seventh (the double oval): used in G and S, which require a compound oval movement.

Work through the families in order. For each family: practise the principal stroke in isolation on a full line, then add the finishing strokes to produce the simplest letter in the family, then work through the remaining letters in the same family. Move to the next family only when the principal stroke feels consistent across a full practice line.

attrs.heading: Common errors in Spencerian capitals
attrs.intro: Problems that appear when learning the capitals alongside the minuscule.

items.symptom: Capitals look much smaller than the minuscule letters they accompany
items.cause: Spencerian capitals are written at 7 to 8 nib widths, the same as the ascender height of the minuscule. If the minuscule ascenders are tall, the capitals should match them.
items.fix: Rule an explicit cap-height guideline at 7 nib widths and follow it for capitals.

items.symptom: The large stem looks stiff rather than flowing
items.cause: The arm is moving too slowly, not allowing the natural curve of the wrist to produce the reverse-curve shape.
items.fix: Speed up the stroke slightly. The reverse curve is produced by wrist movement: the hand rotates slightly outward on the way down. Practise the stroke off the pen first, tracing the shape in the air.
